metadata.title: Government Investment in R&D and Innovation
metadata.description: Government R&D spending, total GERD, and government support for business innovation across G7 countries

sources.name: OECD Main Science and Technology Indicators (MSTI)
sources.notes: Primary source for internationally comparable GERD data. Updated March and September. Uses Frascati Manual 2015 definitions. Data for 2023 is latest available for most countries.

sources.name: ONS UK GERD Statistics
sources.notes: Official UK R&D data. CRITICAL: 2018 methodology change revised UK figures upward. Pre-2018 UK data uses old methodology and is not comparable. Post-2018 data captures previously undercounted business and HE R&D.

sources.name: ONS UK Government SET Expenditure
sources.notes: UK government net expenditure on R&D (SET = Science, Engineering and Technology). Covers departmental R&D spending, UKRI, and research councils. 2023: GBP 17.4bn, up 8.2% from GBP 16.1bn in 2022.

sources.name: OECD R&D Tax Incentives Database
sources.notes: Tracks government tax relief for R&D expenditure and direct government funding of business R&D (BERD). Tax incentive data as % of GDP. 55% of total OECD business R&D support now comes via tax incentives.

sources.name: OECD GBARD Statistics
sources.notes: Government Budget Allocations for R&D. Measures what governments PLAN to spend on R&D. Differs from GERD which measures actual expenditure. GBARD growth slowed to 0.6% in 2023 (from 5.5% in 2022) as COVID stimulus ended.

sources.name: Eurostat GBARD
sources.notes: EU GBARD data. EU total 2024: EUR 127.9bn = 0.71% of GDP. 2023: EUR 123.7bn = 0.73% of GDP (+5.3% YoY).

sources.name: NSF Science & Engineering Indicators 2025
sources.notes: US National Science Foundation biennial report. Provides cross-national comparison of R&D spending including government funding shares. Most recent edition uses 2021-2022 data.

crossCheckNotes.gerdMethodology: GERD figures from OECD, World Bank, and NSF all derive from the same underlying national statistical office data collected under Frascati Manual guidelines. Minor differences arise from: (1) GDP revision timing, (2) PPP vs current prices, (3) rounding. We use OECD as primary source. Where OECD 2023 is unavailable, we use 2022 World Bank/NSF figures.
crossCheckNotes.ukMethodologyBreak: UK GERD data has a major discontinuity at 2018. Pre-2018: ~1.6-1.7% GDP (old methodology). Post-2018: ~2.6-2.9% GDP (revised methodology). This is NOT a real increase — the revision better captured business R&D (surveys expanded) and HE sector spending (new data linkage). We separate pre-2018 and post-2018 UK data and clearly label the break.
crossCheckNotes.govFundingShare: Government share of total GERD varies significantly by country: US ~20%, UK ~15%, Germany/France ~30%. This reflects different innovation models. The US and UK rely more on business-funded R&D and tax incentives; Germany/France have larger direct government funding programs. Neither model is inherently superior.
crossCheckNotes.taxIncentivesVsDirectFunding: OECD tracks two channels of government support for business R&D: (1) direct grants/contracts, typically ~0.10% GDP across OECD, (2) tax incentives, averaging ~0.13% GDP but with wide variation. UK is 4th highest for tax incentives (0.30% GDP) but this is offset by relatively lower direct funding. Total combined support matters more than the channel split.
crossCheckNotes.gbardVsGerd: GBARD (budget allocations) measures government intent; GERD by funding source measures actual spending. These can diverge: GBARD may include allocations not yet spent, while GERD captures what was actually performed. We present GBARD for government commitment comparison and GERD for actual R&D intensity.

gerdByCountryTimeSeries.description: Total Gross Expenditure on R&D as % of GDP — G7 time series
gerdByCountryTimeSeries.sourceName: OECD MSTI / World Bank / NSF S&E Indicators
gerdByCountryTimeSeries.methodologyNote: GERD includes all R&D performed domestically across 4 sectors: business, government, higher education, private non-profit. Uses Frascati Manual definitions. UK pre-2018 figures use old ONS methodology; post-2018 use revised methodology. These are not directly comparable (see crossCheckNotes).

series.notes: NSF/NCSES. $823bn absolute.
OECD preliminary.
OECD. Growth of 2.7% in 2023.
OECD. Growth of 0.8% in 2023.
OECD reports 2.98%. Note: Some sources report ~2.2% using different GDP measures. OECD MSTI 2025 preliminary figure.
Old ONS methodology.
Last year of old methodology.
Revised methodology. NOT a real increase from 1.73%.
OECD. COVID reduced GDP denominator.
ONS confirmed. GBP 70.7bn total.
ONS. GBP 72.6bn total. Below G7 average.
Latest available. 2023 not yet published.
OECD. R&D declined 3.8% in 2023.

govShareOfGerd.description: Government-funded share of total GERD by country (latest available year)
govShareOfGerd.methodologyNote: Percentage of total GERD funded by the government sector. Includes direct government expenditure on R&D and government grants/contracts to other sectors. Does NOT include tax incentives (these reduce tax revenue, not direct spending). Higher share does not necessarily mean more total R&D — it reflects the funding model.

series.notes: High direct state funding model (CNRS, CEA, etc).
Strong public research institutes (Max Planck, Fraunhofer, Helmholtz).
Relatively high gov share of smaller total.
Large absolute amount despite lower share. Federal R&D ~$170bn.
Business-funded model. Government share among lowest in G7.
Lowest G7 alongside Japan. GBP 3.6bn government sector R&D out of GBP 70.7bn total.

ukGovRdSpending.description: UK government net expenditure on R&D (Science, Engineering and Technology)
ukGovRdSpending.methodologyNote: Net government expenditure on science, engineering and technology (SET). Covers central government departments, devolved administrations, UKRI, and research councils. Includes capital and current expenditure. GBP in current prices.

series.notes: Increase includes COVID-related research.
+8.2% YoY. Record high in nominal terms.

govSupportForBusinessRd.description: Government support for business R&D — tax incentives and direct funding as % of GDP
govSupportForBusinessRd.methodologyNote: Combined government support for business R&D through two channels: (1) R&D tax incentives (tax relief for R&D expenditure), (2) direct government funding of BERD (grants, procurement, contracts). OECD average: ~0.23% GDP (0.13% tax + 0.10% direct). 55% of OECD business R&D support now comes via tax incentives. Data for 2022-2023.

series.notes: CIR (Credit Impot Recherche) is Europe's most generous R&D tax credit.
4th highest OECD for tax incentives. R&D Tax Relief + RDEC schemes.
Higher direct funding (SBIR/STTR, DARPA, NIH grants). Lower tax incentive share.
R&D tax incentive only introduced 2020 (Forschungszulage). Strong direct support tradition.
